perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and all dis putes arising under or in connection with this Agreement shall be resolved in accordance with the laws of England and Wales.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of England and Wales. All disputes will be subject to alternative dispute resolution methods as mutually agreed to by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the wording from 'resolved in accordance with the laws of England and Wales' to 'alternative dispute resolution methods' replaces a specific jurisdiction with a vaguely defined process. This can make it difficult to enforce the original intent of the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 may allow third parties to enforce terms of a contract if the contract expressly provides for it, or the term purports to confer a benefit on the third party. By making dispute resolution subject to mutual agreement, it creates uncertainty about which laws and methods will be used, potentially hindering a third party’s ability to enforce rights under the original governing law (England and Wales).
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that disputes will be resolved in accordance with the laws of England and Wales, providing a clear legal framework. The changed text replaces this with 'alternative dispute resolution methods as mutually agreed to by both parties,' which introduces ambiguity and uncertainty about the applicable laws and procedures. While the absence of law snippets makes direct comparison impossible, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that this change weakens the enforceability of the contract under the original governing law. It opens the door for disputes to be resolved outside the legal framework of England and Wales, potentially disadvantaging third parties or undermining the initial intent of the agreement. Given that the structural flaw introduces ambiguity that can be difficult to enforce under state or national law, it creates a contradiction, as it leaves legal obligations open to dispute.

perturbation.original_text: a. the Receiving Party shall not disclose the Material to any Third Party except with the Steering Committee’s prior written consent an d provided that no othe r legal disclosure requirement applies;
perturbation.changed_text: a. the Receiving Party shall not disclose the Material to any Third Party except with the Steering Committee’s prior written consent;
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'provided that no other legal disclosure requirement applies' eliminates an important carve-out for legally mandated disclosures. This can create a situation where the contract appears to prevent a party from complying with a legal obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause acknowledges precedence of legal obligations to disclose. The revised version seems to omit this consideration, placing the receiving party in potential conflict with EU directives. For example, some EU regulations may require disclosure of certain materials despite the NDA. Removing the exception could imply breach of contract if the receiving party complies with the EU law.

the Treaty ( 3 ) , Whereas: (1) Under the Treaty the Council may, by means of directives, adopt minimum requirements for encouraging improvements, especially in the working environment, to guarantee a better level of protection of the health and safety of workers. Such directives are to avoid imposing administrative, financial and legal constraints in a way which would hold back the creation and development of small and medium-sized undertakings. perturbation.justification: The original text includes a carve-out for legal disclosure requirements, ensuring compliance with laws that may mandate disclosure. The changed text removes this carve-out, potentially forcing the Receiving Party to choose between breaching the contract and violating a legal obligation. The Law Explanation notes that some EU regulations may require disclosure of certain materials, and removing the exception could lead to a breach of contract if the receiving party complies with the EU law. While the scraped snippet relates to worker safety and electromagnetic fields, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation clearly highlight the potential contradiction arising from removing the legal disclosure exception. The removed clause directly acknowledges the precedence of legal obligations, and its absence creates a direct contradiction because the contract, as modified, could be interpreted as prohibiting legally required disclosures.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall survive until the earlier of (i) such time as all the material disclosed hereunder becomes public ly known pursuant to Section 15 of the Consortium Agreement and made generally available without vi olation of this Agreement, or (i i) twelve (12) years from date of Registration.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall survive until the earlier of (i) such time as all the material disclosed hereunder becomes public ly known pursuant to Section 15 of the Consortium Agreement and made generally available without vi olation of this Agreement, or (i i) twenty (20) years from date of Registration.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the survival term from 12 years to 20 years impacts the duration of the NDA. It can be unenforceable under different jurisdictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: In England and Wales, the Limitation Act 1980 generally sets a limitation period of six years for actions founded on simple contract. Changing the NDA's survival term to 20 years for actions after registration, may make the clause unenforceable, as it purports to extend the period during which a breach of contract claim can be brought beyond the statutory limitation period.
perturbation.justification: The original contract has a survival term of 12 years, while the changed contract extends it to 20 years. The Law Explanation states that the Limitation Act 1980 in England and Wales generally sets a limitation period of six years for actions founded on simple contract. Thus, changing the NDA's survival term to 20 years for actions after registration could make the clause unenforceable, since it may extend the period during which a breach of contract claim can be brought beyond the statutory limitation period. The scraped snippets are missing, but the Law Explanation and Perturbation explanation point towards the conclusion that there is a contradiction because the contract survival period has been extended beyond the typical statute of limitations, which could make the term unenforceable.
